One-paragraph takeaway. The competition is a per-well sequence-to-sequence regression: predict the true vertical thickness (TVT) of a horizontal wellbore from the Prediction Start point to the toe, using the wellbore trajectory (X, Y, Z), a noisy gamma-ray log (GR, ~29% missing on average), and a paired vertical reference log (typewell) of GR vs TVT. The median well asks the model to extend the last ~27% of known TVT into the next ~73%; geology dips are mostly mild (median |dTVT/dMD| ≈ 0.020) but the upper tail is steep (p95 ≈ 0.03). Naive constant extrapolation lands at a median RMSE of 10.7 ft per well; a 50-point linear-Z extrapolation already cuts the median to 35.4 ft — any modeling effort needs to beat this floor.

2. Dataset Inventory

Two CSVs per well are provided: a horizontal well log sampled every 1 ft of measured depth (MD), and a paired typewell (vertical reference well) sampled every 0.5 ft of TVT. Training wells additionally include a PNG visualisation.

The 3 wells in test/ are a submission template; Kaggle replaces them with a hidden test set at scoring time. Any modeling pipeline must be agnostic to well IDs, well count, and well length.

3. Schema

filecolumnsmissing in test?role
horizontal_well.csvMD, X, Y, Z, GR, TVT_input—features at prediction time
horizontal_well.csv (train only)TVTremoved in testregression target
horizontal_well.csv (train only)ANCC, ASTNU, ASTNL, EGFDU, EGFDL, BUDAremoved in testper-row elevation of formation tops — encodes the true geological structure along the trajectory
typewell.csvTVT, GR, Geology—vertical reference: GR↔TVT dictionary + (sparse) geology label per row

Coordinate notes

  • MD is the wellbore length from surface (ft). Step is uniformly 1.0 ft per row in every well in the dataset.
  • X, Y are projected map coordinates (ft); Z is elevation (negative numbers; deeper = more negative).
  • TVT is true vertical thickness measured in the typewell's vertical reference frame — it is NOT identical to depth-from-surface. It is the depth axis on which the typewell's GR/geology are indexed.
  • The 6 formation-top columns are elevations of geological horizons at the trajectory point (negative, in feet). They differ from TVT by a per-region datum offset.

4. Prediction-Start (PS) Anatomy

In training, TVT_input is set up exactly like the test set: it copies TVT until the PS row, then becomes NaN. This means every training well is a self-contained validation example for the exact prediction task.

The distribution is concentrated: PS sits near the 20–30% mark of the well's MD range, leaving 60–90% of the trajectory (typically ~73%) as the evaluation zone. The known segment is roughly 1,300–2,000 rows in most wells.

5. Target: TVT

TVT is complete in training (0% missing). Per-well range and per-step gradient distributions:

The geology along any single well is not monotonic: every well contains significant fractions of increasing, decreasing, and nearly-flat segments. A model that assumes monotonic TVT will fail on the steep tail.

6. Gamma Ray (GR)

GR is the main signal for geological alignment, and it is noisy:

Most wells have many short missing runs (median run length 1–3 rows) plus a long tail of wells that contain large contiguous gaps. Mean-imputation is dangerous; treat missingness with an explicit mask or use a Gaussian-process / spline imputation that respects MD ordering.

Does GR roughness correlate with steeper geology?

Weak positive trend: wells with more variable GR also tend to traverse steeper geology, but the relationship is far from clean. Don't use GR std alone as a difficulty proxy.

7. Trajectory & Spatial Layout

The wells cluster in a relatively dense region of map space — offset-well analysis is feasible. Lateral azimuths concentrate on two main directions:

This bimodality is consistent with two dominant horizontal-drilling orientations in the survey area. For an inference-time well, the azimuth of nearby training wells is a useful prior on local geological dip.

Test wells vs train-well centroids

8. Typewell Reference Log

Each horizontal well has one paired typewell. Typewell rows are sampled at 0.5 ft of TVT.

GR_range 773.00 174.41 38.08 93.29 120.01 145.22 175.83 195.56 234.91 399.77
geo_unique 773.00 6.77 1.71 4.00 6.00 6.00 6.00 7.00 10.00 21.00

Geology labels are sparse (median ~35% missing — only present at formation-top rows). Across the 773 training typewells the geology vocabulary is small (28 unique labels), dominated by six layers that exactly match the six formation-top column names in the training horizontal CSVs:

9. Formation-Top Columns (Training-Only Leak)

The columns ANCC, ASTNU, ASTNL, EGFDU, EGFDL, BUDA are present only in training. Each row contains the absolute elevation of one geological horizon at the trajectory point. They essentially encode the true geological structure that the model must predict, so they cannot be used as inference inputs — but they are extremely useful as auxiliary regression targets in a multi-task setup, or for building synthetic supervision signals.

Median d(formation-top)/dMD values are tightly centered near zero with long tails — consistent with mostly-flat geology that occasionally dips. Critically, all six layers move together: at any given training row the six layer elevations are highly correlated, since they describe the same dipping stack of beds.

10. Sample Well Walkthrough

Three demo wells across the lateral-length distribution (short / median / long evaluation zone). Each row shows TVT vs MD with the known/unknown split, the horizontal GR vs MD, and the typewell GR–TVT dictionary with the known horizontal GR overlaid.

11. Baseline RMSE Floors

To establish what the metric looks like before any modeling, we simulate the official task on every training well using its own PS index. Three baselines:

  • constant: predict the last-known TVT for all post-PS rows.
  • linear in MD: fit TVT ~ a + b·MD on the last 50 known rows, extrapolate forward.
  • linear in Z: fit TVT ~ a + b·Z on the last 50 known rows; uses the wellbore's own vertical motion as a proxy for geological dip.
  • oracle constant (diagnostic): predict the mean of the true post-PS TVT — the lowest RMSE any constant predictor could achieve. Gap to constant tells you how much "drift" is in the truth.
baseline count mean std min 50% 75% 90% 95% max
rmse_constant 773.00 12.81 8.90 1.12 10.67 15.81 22.97 29.01 70.64
rmse_linear_md 773.00 60.70 87.28 1.93 38.01 79.21 132.29 167.20 1,392.70
rmse_linear_z 773.00 117.38 304.69 0.98 35.44 80.47 254.08 468.54 4,242.26
rmse_oracle_const 773.00 7.85 4.31 1.01 6.91 9.52 13.11 16.11 33.53
What this means for modelling.
  • The linear-Z baseline cuts median RMSE roughly in half versus constant — the wellbore's Z trajectory is already a strong predictor of TVT drift, since the lateral often roughly parallels the bed.
  • The gap between constant and oracle-constant is large, confirming that the truth drifts during the eval zone — "predict last known value" is leaving a lot of error on the table.
  • Any serious model must beat the linear-Z floor on a leave-one-well-out CV. If it doesn't, it hasn't learned anything beyond local geometry.

12. Modeling Recommendations

  1. Validation: leave-one-well-out (or GroupKFold by XY cluster) on the 773 training wells is the only credible CV. Random row-shuffles leak because rows within a well are highly autocorrelated.
  2. Per-well baseline ensemble: even a simple weighted blend of constant + linear-Z is a competitive starting submission and gives a useful sanity check for any learned model.
  3. Signal alignment: dynamic time warping (or cross-correlation) of the post-PS horizontal GR against either (a) the pre-PS HW GR↔TVT_input dictionary or (b) the typewell GR↔TVT curve gives a per-step TVT estimate. The slides emphasize that the pre-PS HW dictionary is often better than the typewell because the horizontal GR has higher resolution.
  4. Sequence model: 1-D CNN or bi-directional Transformer that ingests (MD-MD_PS, ΔX, ΔY, ΔZ, GR, GR_mask, TVT_input, TVT_input_mask, typewell_GR_at_TVT_input) and predicts TVT in absolute or delta form. Multi-task auxiliary heads on the six formation-top elevations stabilize representations during training and add no inference-time cost.
  5. Spatial prior: for each test well, find the K nearest train wells by XY centroid; their TVT curves (after aligning on the shared TVT range) provide a strong dip prior. Especially useful for wells where GR is mostly missing.
  6. Submission packaging: the visible test folder is a template only — load test wells by directory listing, not hard-coded IDs. Don't tune any heuristics to those 3 wells.

13. Companion Files

The report script also emits these CSVs into report/ for reuse:

  • well_summary.csv — one row per (train+test) horizontal well, ~30 numeric features.
  • typewell_summary.csv — one row per training typewell.
  • geology_counts.csv — geology label frequency across all training typewells.
  • baseline_rmse.csv — per-well RMSE for the three simulated baselines, useful as a CV target distribution.
  • test_nearest_neighbours.csv — 5 nearest train wells (by XY centroid) for each visible test well.
